original_abstract:
This article examines the operational landscape of the administrative judiciary in Jordan following its transition from a single-tier system—previously overseen by the High Court of Justice—to a dual-level structure comprising the Administrative Court and the Supreme Administrative Court. This structural shift has significantly bolstered public confidence by aligning the Jordanian legal system with international standards for fair trials, which emphasize the right to have a case reviewed by more than one judicial level. Through an empirical analysis of quantitative data, this study highlights a notable increase in the volume of administrative litigation in 2020 compared to 2019. 

Despite these advancements, the study identifies a critical geographic limitation: administrative courts of first instance remain localized in the capital, rather than being distributed across the Hashemite Kingdom. To address this, the paper advocates for the decentralization of the administrative judiciary through the establishment of first-instance and appellate courts in all governorates. Furthermore, the study underscores the need for a standardized jurisdictional framework in dual-system countries. Such a standard is essential for clearly distinguishing between the general jurisdiction of ordinary courts and the specialized jurisdiction of administrative courts, thereby eliminating procedural ambiguity in administrative disputes.
humanized_abstract:
The Jordanian administrative judiciary has gone through significant developments in the way it operates since its evolution from the High Court of Justice into the Administrative Court and the Supreme Administrative Court. As a result, Justice has been served to the public in a more professional manner that concedes to the international minimum requirements of the principle of the trial before several judicial instances. This paper investigates the developments of the administrative judiciary after the transformation that the Jordanian judiciary as a whole has gone through. It relies on empirical results gathered from quantitative data that show the rise of the number of administrative suits in 2020 compared with the number of administrative suits in 2019.

Although the administration judiciary in Jordan has undergone many developments, the study revealed that there is a gap in the administrative judiciary, which is the geographic location of the administrative courts of first instance only in Amman, the capital, rather than spreading them across the Hashemite Kingdom. Therefore, the study recommends the decentralization of the administrative judiciary by establishing first instance and appeal courts in all the governorates. In addition, the study recommends the adoption of a uniform judicial structure in dual system of justice, which ensures the principles of discrimination between the general jurisdiction of the ordinary courts and the specialized judicial competence of the administrative judiciary. Therefore, the ambiguity of the procedures of administrative disputes would be eliminated, as they are submitted to ordinary courts without specifying the competent judicial body to settle them.
